cataloging_subject.label: Zwei mittelenglische Bearbeitungen der Sage von St. Patrik's Purgatorium
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edited composite represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00066: Eugen Kölbing's 1877 presentation of two distinct anonymous Middle English verse versions of the St Patrick's Purgatory narrative, separated by his German textual commentary and followed by a German typographic note and editorial dateline. This record does not collapse the two medieval versions, their manuscript witnesses, the Latin source tradition, Kölbing's complete article, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one homogeneous medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: Zwei mittelenglische Bearbeitungen der Sage von St. Patrik's Purgatorium
catalog_summary.author: Two anonymous Middle English adapters
catalog_summary.date: Version I attested c. 1331–1340; Version II attested in later fifteenth-century witnesses; edited composite published 1877
catalog_summary.region: England; the precise places of composition are unresolved, while the surviving witnesses belong to distinct London and later medieval English production contexts

titles.value: Zwei mittelenglische Bearbeitungen der Sage von St. Patrik's Purgatorium
Two Middle English Adaptations of the Legend of St Patrick's Purgatory
notes: Discovery translation of the preferred German title; it is not presented as a historical title.
Kölbing brackets the heading because the manuscript beginning and title are lost.
The exact intermediary between this Middle English version and the Latin St Patrick's Purgatory tradition remains unresolved.

scope.description: Manuscript readings, notes, and corrections in the 1877 edited presentation

date_statements.display: Kölbing dated the edited composite Breslau, October 1876
Kölbing's edited composite was published in 1877
notes: This is the publication date of the cataloging subject, not the composition date of either medieval version.

date_statements.display: Version I is attested in the Auchinleck manuscript, probably produced c. 1331–1340
notes: The date belongs to the surviving witness and supplies a terminus ante quem, not an exact composition date.

date_statements.display: Version II is attested in later fifteenth-century copies, including Cotton MS Caligula A II (1450–1499) and Beinecke MS 365 (fourth quarter of the fifteenth century)
notes: The range summarizes witness attestation and is not narrowed into an unsupported composition date.
Breslau is an editorial place, not a medieval place of composition.

language_statements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is embedded or editorial rather than the primary language of either poem.

form_statements.description: Two Middle English verse texts with substantive German prose commentary and a final prose editorial note
Version I is a tail-rhyme poem; Version II is in rhyming couplets

summaries.value: Kölbing presents two anonymous Middle English verse tellings of Sir Owayne's journey through St Patrick's Purgatory. Version I, incomplete at the beginning in the Auchinleck manuscript, is arranged in 198 tail-rhyme stanzas. German notes then discuss readings and the relation between the versions. Version II, transmitted here from Cotton MS Caligula A II, narrates the same otherworld journey in rhyming couplets and ends with the knight's return, later pilgrimage, penitential life, and salvation. A short German note explains typographic signs in the transcription.

The encoding begins within printed p. 98 at Version I and continues through the signature on p. 121. It omits Kölbing's article introduction on pp. 57–98, including the prose above Version I on p. 98, but retains both edited poems, the intervening commentary, the final typographic note, and the Breslau/October 1876 dateline and signature.

extent.description: Four encoded content parts: Version I with 198 line groups and 1,188 L elements, 22 German commentary paragraphs, Version II with one line group and 682 L elements, and one final German note paragraph. These are XML representation metrics, not normalized critical line totals.

content_parts.label: I. [Purgatorium Sancti Patricii]
content_parts.summary: Anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme version from the Auchinleck manuscript, lacking its beginning and manuscript title.
notes: The XML has 1,188 L elements because the first encoded stanza includes two dotted placeholder lines for lost text; the current NLS project heading reports 1,186 lines in Easting's lineation.

content_parts.label: Anmerkungen
content_parts.summary: Kölbing's German textual and comparative notes following Version I.

content_parts.label: II. Owayne Myles
content_parts.summary: Anonymous Middle English couplet version transcribed by Kölbing from Cotton MS Caligula A II.
notes: Kölbing cites legacy f. 89 ff.; the current British Library catalog places Owayne Miles at ff. 91v–95r. The foliation crosswalk remains unresolved.

content_parts.label: Typographic note and editorial dateline
content_parts.summary: German note explaining italic letterforms used to represent manuscript strokes, followed by Breslau, October 1876 and Kölbing's signature.

related_works.title: Tractatus de Purgatorio Sancti Patricii
notes: This relation applies to the two medieval verse parts, not to Kölbing's editorial prose. The exact Latin or vernacular intermediary for either Middle English version is not established here; Kölbing argues only for a fuller shared source, probably Latin.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME00066 source XML and structural inspection
evidence.locator: IDG; REVDESCR; TEXT/@LANG; DIV1 and four DIV2 components; PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, P, LG, L, TRAILER, and CLOSER
evidence.summary: Verifies identifiers, composite title and boundaries, headings, 1,870 verse-line elements in 199 line groups, 23 prose paragraphs, editorial notes, source-page and manuscript milestones, final explicit and dateline, and the 2016 removal of opening prose fragments.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00066 and About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
evidence.summary: Confirms stable U-M item identity and public collection context; the collection page describes manual keying from public-domain editions, in-house proofing and editing, and basic TEI encoding.

evidence.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Zwei mittelenglische Bearbeitungen der Sage von St. Patrik's Purgatorium,' Englische Studien 1 (1877), 57–121
evidence.locator: article pp. 57–121; encoded unit pp. 98–121 (PDF pages 110–133)
evidence.summary: Direct inspection verifies bibliographic identity, Kölbing's source discussion and manuscript use, the two edited texts and intervening notes, exact encoded selection, legacy witness foliation, and the October 1876 dateline.

Kölbing identifies the Auchinleck Owayne Miles locus, cites his 1877 pp. 98–113 edition, and reports corrections from a fresh collation; this later correction list concerns Version I and is not modeled as upstream of the XML.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Adv.MS.19.2.1, and Auchinleck Manuscript project item 'St Patrick's Purgatory and the Knight, Sir Owen'
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark, c. 1331–1340 London witness context, physical extent, restricted original with digital surrogate, and exact Version I locus; the project exposes the transcription and facsimile sequence.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A II and Part 1
evidence.summary: Confirms the current two-part physical object, paper support and 210-folio extent, later-fifteenth-century Part 1, Middle English language, exact current Owayne Miles locus and adjacent works, and absence of a linked digitized object in the exact component record.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Pre-1600 Manuscripts, MS 365 (Book of Brome)
evidence.locator: item 10, Sir Owen the Knight, ff. 28r–38r
evidence.summary: Confirms a fourth-quarter-fifteenth-century collateral copy of the Version II tradition, its exact locus, codex extent and original wrapper, and curator-restricted physical access.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries MARCO, MS. Eng. poet. d. 208
evidence.locator: Part 1, Owayne Miles on fols. 15, 18, 22, 25, 27, and 28
evidence.summary: Confirms a guardbook of recovered Middle English binding fragments, including collateral leaves from the Version I tradition, their exact mounted-leaf loci and binding provenance.

evidence.citation: Robert Easting, An edition of Owayne Miles and other Middle English texts concerning St. Patrick's Purgatory (DPhil thesis, University of Oxford, 1977)
evidence.summary: The official ORA abstract identifies Henry of Saltrey's Latin Tractatus as the principal transmission context, six Middle English verse versions, and the thesis's editions and source study; it supports tradition-level description without making the thesis upstream of Kölbing or the XML.
Provides entity, relation, exact source mapping, complete-object cache, access, rights, editorial-practice, and unresolved-question evidence for this work-level record.

open_questions.question: How does Kölbing's legacy Cotton locus f. 89 ff. crosswalk to the current British Library locator ff. 91v–95r?
next_steps: Obtain or inspect complete Cotton MS Caligula A II images and a repository foliation concordance; retain the current catalog locus as authoritative for discovery meanwhile.

open_questions.question: Can complete, reusable whole-object image sets be obtained for Cotton MS Caligula A II, Beinecke MS 365, and Bodleian MS. Eng. poet. d. 208?
next_steps: Use each repository's mediated reproduction route to request the complete physical object, including binding or wrapper, covers, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, guards, and all leaves, while identifying the target folios separately and obtaining explicit reuse terms.

open_questions.question: What exact intermediary and stemmatic relationships connect the two Middle English versions to each other and to the Latin Tractatus tradition?
next_steps: Review modern critical editions and source studies beyond their public landing-page abstracts; preserve Kölbing's cautious fuller-common-source hypothesis rather than asserting direct derivation between the two versions.

open_questions.question: What item-level U-M production record explains selection of printed pp. 98–121 and removal of the article introduction?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for selection and revision notes while preserving the directly observed XML-to-print mapping and the 2016 revision description.

notes: The two Middle English poems are modeled as distinct anonymous adaptations within a modern edited composite; Kölbing's German commentary is not assigned to either medieval author.
Witness production dates and places are not transferred automatically into medieval composition claims.
The XML's 1,188 Version I L elements include two dotted loss markers and therefore differ from the NLS project's 1,186-line heading; both metrics are preserved in their own representation contexts.
The complete NLS project image inventory is a digital-facsimile access object, not evidence that every physical exterior or preservation master is publicly exposed.
